report:
# "Not Here" — Technical Architecture: One Engine, Two Frontends

## 0. Design thesis

Every hard requirement in the brief — stats that matter, cross-run memory, adaptive leitmotif music, deterministic branching, NG+ awareness, heavy automated testing of story content — points at the same architectural decision: **the engine must be a pure, deterministic state machine over serializable data**. Text output, music, UI, and saves are all *projections* of one `WorldState`. If we get that right, "web + terminal" is trivial; if we get it wrong, nothing else works.

Core loop, conceptually:

```ts
advance(state: WorldState, input: PlayerInput): StepResult
// StepResult = { state: WorldState; output: SceneOutput; events: EngineEvent[] }
```

- Pure function. No I/O, no `Date.now()`, no `Math.random()`, no DOM, no Node APIs.
- `EngineEvent[]` is a typed stream (`text`, `choice-presented`, `music`, `ending-reached`, `memory-recorded`, `achievement`). Frontends and the audio system are just event consumers.
- Immutability throughout (aligns with the house coding rules): every step returns a new state; this is also what makes snapshot testing, time-travel debugging, and save-anywhere free.

---

## 1. Monorepo layout (pnpm workspaces, TypeScript strict everywhere)

```
not-here/
├── pnpm-workspace.yaml
├── package.json                  # scripts: build, test, validate-story, play
├── tsconfig.base.json            # strict, isolatedModules, verbatimModuleSyntax
├── packages/
│   ├── engine/                   # PURE. Zero DOM/Node deps. Enforced (see below).
│   │   └── src/
│   │       ├── state/            # WorldState type, stat defs, memory/flags, reducers
│   │       ├── runtime/          # scene interpreter, choice gating, effects application
│   │       ├── dsl/              # defineScene(), condition & effect expression types
│   │       ├── rng/              # pure-rand wrapper; rng state lives IN WorldState
│   │       ├── save/             # SaveFile schema (Zod), versioning, migrations, replay
│   │       ├── music/            # MusicEvent types + MusicDirector (state → target mix)
│   │       └── events/           # EngineEvent discriminated union
│   ├── story/                    # CONTENT. Depends only on engine's DSL types.
│   │   └── src/
│   │       ├── scenes/           # one file per scene/beat cluster (act1/, act2/, …)
│   │       ├── characters/       # cast definitions, relationship axes, motif tags
│   │       ├── music/            # cue sheet: cues, layers, stingers, motif→character map
│   │       ├── endings/          # ending scene defs + unlock predicates
│   │       └── index.ts          # assembled StoryBundle (validated at build time)
│   ├── audio-assets/             # OGG/MP3 stems + manifest.json (ids ↔ files, loop points)
│   ├── llm/                      # OPTIONAL, isolated. Character-creation call, Zod-validated
│   │                             #   output, deterministic offline fallback. Engine never imports it.
│   └── tooling/                  # story-graph analyzer, walker bots, coverage reporter,
│                                 #   scene-id codegen, save-fixture generator
├── apps/
│   ├── web/                      # Vite + React (thin). WebAudioAdapter, localStorage saves.
│   └── cli/                      # Ink 5 (React for terminals). MpvAudioAdapter, file saves.
└── fixtures/                     # golden playthroughs, versioned save files for migration tests
```

**Purity enforcement for `packages/engine`** (this is the load-bearing rule, so make it mechanical):
- `"types": []` in its tsconfig — no `dom` or `node` lib types compile.
- ESLint `no-restricted-imports` / `import/no-nodejs-modules` in engine + story.
- A CI test that imports the built engine into a bare V8 context (e.g., run under `node --experimental-permission` or just a smoke test asserting no `window`/`process` references in the bundle).

**Dependency direction:** `story → engine (types only)`, `apps → engine + story + platform adapters`, `tooling → engine + story`. `llm` is only imported by apps, never by engine/story — this structurally guarantees the "LLM is limited" requirement.

**Frontend responsibilities are identical and thin:** render `SceneOutput`, collect a choice, call `advance`, dispatch `events` to the audio adapter and save adapter. The web app is Vite + React with a typewriter-text component; the CLI is Ink 5 (Ink is React for terminals, so ~70% of view-model code — choice list, text reveal pacing, status line — can live in a shared `ui-core` hook package if desired; start without it, extract when duplication appears).

Tooling choices: pnpm workspaces (no Turborepo needed at this scale; `pnpm -r --filter` is enough), Vitest for all tests (runs engine tests in node, no browser needed), `tsx` for tooling scripts, Changesets only if you ever publish.

---

## 2. Narrative format decision (the big one)

Honest evaluation against the four criteria that matter here: (a) stats/memory as first-class state, (b) adaptive-music hooks, (c) testability/static analysis, (d) authoring ergonomics for an AI writing lots of content.

### inkjs (Inkle's ink)
- **Strengths:** The best prose-branching *language* ever shipped. Weaves, gathers, glue, and inline alternates make text variation dirt cheap — this is where custom engines usually feel worse. inkjs is mature, includes a JS compiler (no C# inklecate needed), runs fine in browser and Node. State is exposed: `story.variablesState`, `ObserveVariable` callbacks, external functions, and knot/line tags (`# music: ward_theme`) — you *can* wire stats and music cues.
- **Weaknesses, honestly:**
  - **State is stringly-typed.** Ink variables are untyped ints/strings/floats in a flat namespace. A rich `WorldState` (per-character relationship axes, memory ledger, NG+ meta) becomes dozens of loose globals with zero compile-time checking. The AI author's best validation loop — the TypeScript compiler — is gone; errors surface at runtime mid-playthrough.
  - **Save fragility.** Ink save state (`story.state.ToJson()`) is tightly coupled to compiled story structure. Edit a knot, old saves break. Ink has no migration story; for a game in continuous authoring this is a real operational pain, and it collides directly with the schema-versioning requirement.
  - **Opaque to analysis.** Compiled ink JSON is an interpreter bytecode, not a graph you can trivially BFS. Reachability/orphan analysis means either simulation-only testing or writing a compiled-ink static analyzer (nontrivial).
  - Memory-across-runs and NG+ require shuttling state in/out of ink via external functions — the interesting state ends up living *outside* ink anyway, at which point ink is only providing prose flow.
- **Verdict:** Best-in-class for prose, weakest of the serious options for stats/memory/saves/analysis. Viable, but you'd fight it exactly where this game is ambitious.

### Twine / Twee (SugarCube, Harlowe)
- Story formats assume a DOM; the "engine" is the HTML runtime. To meet "one pure engine, terminal build," you'd write a custom Twee interpreter — i.e., a custom engine with a worse authoring format. Twee's macros are stringly, untestable, and unpleasant for programmatic authoring. **Eliminate.**

### Yarn Spinner (JS: yarn-bound / bondage.js)
- Lovely dialogue model, `<<command>>` syntax maps nicely to music cues, declared-type variables. But the canonical implementation is C#/Unity; the JS ports are community-maintained, lag the language spec, and have small ecosystems. Betting the whole game on a second-tier port of a dialogue-first (not stat-first) tool is the wrong risk. **Eliminate.**

### Custom TypeScript scene-graph DSL — **recommended**
Scenes are **TypeScript modules exporting data**, built with a typed helper, so we get code-level ergonomics with data-level analyzability:

```ts
// packages/story/src/scenes/act1/ward-corridor.ts
export default defineScene({
  id: 'act1/ward-corridor',
  music: { cue: 'ward_theme', intensity: stat('dread') },        // declarative music hook
  text: [
    line`The corridor hums. ${ifFlag('met_mara', 'Mara's door is ajar.', 'Every door is shut.')}`,
    whenStat('perception', { gte: 3 }, line`One nameplate is blank. It wasn't yesterday.`),
  ],
  choices: [
    {
      id: 'knock', label: 'Knock on the blank door',
      require: all(stat('nerve', { gte: 2 }), notFlag('door_refused')),
      effects: [addStat('dread', 1), setFlag('knocked_blank_door'), stinger('motif_absence')],
      goto: 'act1/the-empty-room',
    },
    { id: 'leave', label: 'Walk away', effects: [remember('avoided_blank_door')], goto: 'act1/nurse-station' },
  ],
});
```

Key design rules that make this work:
1. **Conditions and effects are serializable expression objects, not closures** (`all/any/not`, `stat/flag/memory/visited/ending-seen` predicates; `addStat/setFlag/remember/goto/stinger` effects). This is what makes static reachability analysis, save-replay, and diffing possible. Provide a `custom(fn)` escape hatch, but the validator flags every use and requires simulation coverage for those edges.
2. **Scene IDs are checked at compile time.** A codegen step (`pnpm gen`) emits a `SceneId` string-literal union from the scenes directory; `goto: 'act1/the-empty-roon'` is a *type error*. Same for flag names, stat names, cue names, motif names — all closed unions generated from registries. For an AI author producing volume, this converts the #1 bug class (dangling references, typo'd flags) into red squiggles before anything runs.
3. **Prose ergonomics recovered via a micro-templating layer** — the `line`/`ifFlag`/`whenStat`/`cycle`/`once` combinators above give you ~85% of ink's weave power (conditional text, variations, first-visit-only lines) inside type-checked TS. You will not match ink's full weave/gather flow; accept that, and compensate by making scenes small and cheap to create (many small files — which also matches the house style rules).
4. **Why this fits an AI author best:** LLMs are excellent at producing structured TS against a well-typed API, and the compiler + validator become an automatic red/green loop (author scene → `tsc` + `validate-story` → fix). With ink, the equivalent loop is compile + *play through it* to find state bugs.
5. **Cost:** you own a runtime. Realistically the interpreter is 300–600 lines (evaluate conditions, apply effects, resolve text, emit events) plus the DSL types. That's days, not weeks, and every requirement on the list (music events, NG+ meta-state, save replay, deterministic RNG threading) gets a first-class home instead of a workaround.

**Runner-up / hybrid note:** if prose volume ever makes the combinators painful, the escape valve is embedding ink *fragments* for individual scenes' text while keeping graph/state/music in the TS engine — inkjs used as a text-realization library, not the state machine. Don't start there; it doubles the toolchain.

---

## 3. Save system

**Everything serializable, always.** `WorldState` is plain JSON data (no class instances, no Maps unless via codec, no functions) validated by a Zod schema — Zod gives runtime validation at the load boundary (never trust file/localStorage content) plus inferred TS types.

```ts
interface SaveFile {
  schemaVersion: number;          // save format version
  engineVersion: string;          // package version, informational
  storyHash: string;              // content hash of the built StoryBundle
  slot: string;                   // 'slot-1'…'slot-N', 'autosave', 'quicksave'
  createdAt: string; updatedAt: string;
  seed: string;                   // run seed
  rngState: RngState;             // serialized PRNG state
  state: WorldState;              // scene pointer, stats, flags, memory ledger, visit counts
  choiceLog: ChoiceRecord[];      // ordered list of every input this run
}
```

- **`choiceLog` is the crown jewel.** Because the engine is deterministic (pure + seeded RNG), a save can *always* be reconstructed by replaying the log through the current story content. When content changes and `storyHash` mismatches, attempt replay-migration: re-run the log; if every choice still resolves, you've upgraded the save "for free"; if a choice no longer exists, fall back to nearest checkpoint and tell the player honestly. This is precisely the story ink can't offer, and for a game under continuous authoring it's the difference between "patch breaks saves" and not.
- **Schema versioning:** `schemaVersion` + a linear chain of pure migration functions (`migrateV1toV2(save): SaveV2`), each with fixture-based tests (`fixtures/saves/v1/*.json` must load in every future version). Never mutate in place; migrations return new objects.
- **Two persistence scopes, deliberately separate:**
  - **Run saves** (slots, per-playthrough).
  - **`MetaState`** — the cross-run ledger: endings seen, key deeds witnessed, NG+ flags, "the game remembers you" data. Stored in its own versioned file/key so deleting a run never deletes the game's memory of you (and so the twist/NG+ layer can read it on a fresh run). This is the mechanical substrate for requirement #4 and the retrospective-clue design in #5.
- **Storage adapters** (interface in engine; implementations in apps):
  - Web: `localStorage` (with size guard ~5MB; choiceLogs are small text, fine) behind `StorageAdapter`; trivially swappable to IndexedDB later.
  - CLI: JSON files under the platform data dir via `env-paths` (`~/Library/Application Support/not-here/` on macOS, XDG on Linux), atomic writes (write temp + rename).
  - Bonus: identical `SaveFile` JSON means saves are portable between web and terminal — a genuinely cool player-facing feature you get for free.

---

## 4. Deterministic seeded RNG

- Use **`pure-rand`** (the immutable PRNG library underneath fast-check): xoroshiro128+/mersenne generators, pure API — `next()` returns `[value, nextRngState]`. This matches the immutability rule and integrates directly with property testing later.
- **RNG state lives inside `WorldState`** and is threaded through every reducer; `Math.random` is banned by lint in engine/story. Serialization is automatic because it's just state.
- Seed originates at character creation (player-visible, shareable — speedrunners and testers will thank you). Same seed + same choiceLog ⇒ bit-identical playthrough on both platforms; this invariant is itself a CI test.
- Use RNG sparingly and *diegetically* (flavor variation, minor bark selection), never for branch-critical outcomes without a stat gate — determinism of the *story* is a design requirement, not just a technical one.

---

## 5. Testing story content (the #1 risk, treated as such)

Three layers, all runnable via `pnpm validate-story` and gating CI:

**Layer 1 — Static graph analysis (fast, exact where possible).** Because scenes/choices/conditions are data, `tooling/` builds the directed graph and checks:
- Every scene reachable from `start` (ignoring conditions) — catches orphans.
- Every declared ending reachable; every non-ending scene has ≥1 outgoing edge — catches dead ends.
- No dangling `goto`/flag/stat/cue references (also caught at compile time via codegen unions — belt and suspenders).
- Condition sanity: flags that are read but never set (and vice versa), stat gates outside the stat's declared range, choices whose `require` is provably unsatisfiable for trivially decidable predicates.
- Output: a DOT/Mermaid export of the graph for human review of structure.

**Layer 2 — Simulation bots (probabilistic, catches conditional reachability).** Exact reachability under conditions is state-explosion-hard; don't pretend otherwise — simulate:
- **Random walkers:** N=10k+ seeded playthroughs (pure engine ⇒ thousands per second, no UI). Assert: every run terminates within a step budget (infinite-loop detection), never throws, stats stay in bounds.
- **Coverage report:** % of scenes / choices / endings / music cues hit across the walker corpus. CI fails if any ending is never reached or coverage regresses below threshold — this is the practical answer to "is ending 7 actually reachable with the conditions as authored."
- **Directed walkers:** greedy bots biased toward unvisited edges and toward extreme stat builds (max-empathy run, max-nerve run) to probe gated content that uniform random walks miss.
- **Property tests (fast-check):** invariants like *save/load mid-walk then continue ≡ uninterrupted walk*, *replay(choiceLog) ≡ original final state*, *advance is pure (same inputs ⇒ same outputs)*.

**Layer 3 — Snapshot & golden-path tests (protects the authored experience).**
- Scripted playthroughs (`fixtures/playthroughs/*.json`: seed + choice sequence) whose full transcript — text, state checkpoints, and **the ordered music-event stream** — is Vitest-snapshotted. Editing act 1 and silently changing act 3's emotional beats or cue sequence shows up as a snapshot diff in review.
- Save-migration fixtures per schema version (from §3).
- One golden path per ending, so every ending's route is executable documentation.

---

## 6. Music events: audio as a projection of the state machine

Music is driven by the **same events on both platforms**; only the last-mile playback differs.

**Content model (in `packages/story/music/`):** the score is authored as **cue sheets of layered, loop-synced stems** (Undertale-style leitmotif discipline): each *cue* (per area/act) has layers (`bed`, `melody`, `tension`, per-character motif stems recorded at the same tempo/key), plus one-shot *stingers*. Each ensemble character owns a motif id; cues declare which motif layers they can host. (This structure is also where the twist can live musically — e.g., a motif layer that is conspicuously *absent* until the reveal: "not here.")

**Engine emission — three sources, all declarative:**
1. **Scene metadata:** `music: { cue: 'ward_theme', intensity: stat('dread') }` → on scene entry the engine emits `{type:'music', op:'cue', cue, transition:'crossfade'}` and a derived intensity value.
2. **State-transition rules** (registered in story, evaluated by engine after every effect application): *when `trust.mara` crosses ≥3 → `{op:'layer', cue:'*', layer:'motif_mara', active:true}`*; *when `dread` changes → `{op:'param', name:'intensity', value}`*. Because these are rules over state diffs, music reacts to *accumulated history*, not just location — the memory system and the score share one brain.
3. **Explicit stingers** in choice effects: `stinger('motif_absence')`.

**Shared `MusicDirector` (in engine or `audio-core`):** pure logic that folds music events + state into a **target mix** `{ cue, layerGains: Record<LayerId, number>, tempoFlags }`, handling debouncing, crossfade scheduling *decisions*, and bar-boundary quantization *requests*. Fully unit-testable with no audio device.

**Platform adapters** consume target mixes:
- **Web:** raw **WebAudio** (not Howler — Howler's sprite model fights sample-synced layer mixing): all stems of the active cue start simultaneously into per-layer `GainNode`s; layer toggles are gain ramps (0→1) so everything stays phase-locked; stingers are one-shot buffers; crossfades between cues are dual-bus gain ramps. This is a well-trodden ~200-line pattern.
- **CLI:** the honest hard part. Recommended: an **`mpv` adapter** — spawn one mpv process per stem (`--loop --input-ipc-server=socket`), start them together, drive per-stem volume over mpv's JSON IPC; stingers via fire-and-forget mpv/`afplay`. mpv is cross-platform, robust, and IPC volume ramps give real adaptive layering in a terminal. Fallbacks in order: (a) `speaker`/naudiodon PCM mixing in-process (perfect sync, but native-module install pain), (b) degrade gracefully to single-track cue swaps via `afplay`/`ffplay`, (c) `--mute` mode with an on-screen "♪ now playing: Mara's Theme (+tension)" line — the event stream renders even without audio, which is also your debugging view.
- Adapter contract is identical, so the golden-path snapshot of the music-event stream (Layer 3 testing) verifies the *score logic* platform-independently.

---

## 7. LLM boundary (limited but meaningful)

- `packages/llm` is imported **only by apps**, never engine/story — the architecture enforces the design pledge.
- Use case 1 (per brief): **character creation** — free-text self-description → Zod-validated `{ statAllocation, epithet, flavorLines[] }`; the engine receives it as ordinary typed input data. Deterministic questionnaire fallback when offline (CLI users especially) — required, since the terminal build must work air-gapped.
- Everything downstream is authored/deterministic; if later you want LLM-flavored *incidental* barks, they must pass through the same schema-validated, engine-as-data boundary.

---

## 8. Concrete stack summary

| Concern | Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Language/build | TypeScript 5 strict, pnpm workspaces, tsup for packages | Types are the AI author's test harness; pnpm filtering is enough, skip Turborepo |
| Narrative | **Custom TS scene-graph DSL** (data-first, codegen'd ID unions, text combinators) | Only option that makes stats/memory/music/saves/analysis first-class; see §2 |
| Web app | Vite + React | Fast, boring, fine |
| CLI app | **Ink 5** | React model shared conceptually with web; solid focus/list/input primitives |
| RNG | `pure-rand` | Immutable API, serializable state, fast-check synergy |
| Validation | Zod (save files, LLM output, story-bundle build check) | Never trust external data |
| Tests | Vitest + fast-check + custom graph analyzer + walker bots | §5; `validate-story` gates CI |
| Web audio | Raw WebAudio, gain-node layer mixing | Sample-synced stems; Howler/Tone unnecessary |
| CLI audio | mpv JSON-IPC adapter (afplay/mute-with-lyrics fallbacks) | Real layered playback in a terminal; graceful degradation |
| Persistence | StorageAdapter → localStorage / env-paths JSON files; choiceLog replay migration | §3; saves portable across platforms |
| LLM | Isolated `packages/llm`, Anthropic API, schema-clamped, offline fallback | Enforces "limited but meaningful" |

**Build order suggestion:** engine core + DSL + 5 toy scenes → CLI frontend (fastest feedback) → validator + walkers → save system → MusicDirector with mute-mode "♪" rendering → web frontend → WebAudio adapter → mpv adapter. Music *events* land early even though audio lands late — the score logic matures with the story.

options.name: Custom TypeScript scene-graph DSL (recommended)
options.summary: Scenes as typed data built with defineScene() helpers; serializable condition/effect expressions; codegen'd SceneId/flag/stat/cue unions; small (~300-600 line) pure interpreter emitting typed events including music.
options.pros: First-class WorldState with full compile-time type safety; conditions-as-data enable exact static graph analysis (orphans, dead ends, dangling refs); choiceLog replay gives save migration across content updates; music hooks are native, not bolted on; NG+ meta-state trivial; TS compiler + validator form an automatic red/green loop for an AI authoring high volume; zero external runtime risk.
options.cons: You own the runtime (days of work, ongoing maintenance); prose-variation ergonomics weaker than ink's weaves/gathers — a micro-templating combinator layer recovers ~85% but dense prose variation is more verbose; risk of DSL scope creep if escape-hatch closures aren't policed.

options.name: inkjs (Inkle's ink)
options.summary: Mature branching-prose language with a full JS compiler/runtime; state exposed via variablesState, variable observers, external functions, and tags usable for music cues.
options.pros: Best-in-class prose authoring (weaves, gathers, glue, inline alternates); battle-tested in shipped games; runs in browser and Node; LLMs know the syntax reasonably well; tags give a workable music-cue channel.
options.cons: Flat, stringly-typed variable namespace — no compile-time checking of the rich stat/memory model, errors surface at runtime; saves are tightly coupled to compiled structure with no migration story (content patches break saves); compiled JSON is interpreter bytecode, effectively opaque to graph reachability analysis; cross-run/NG+ state must live outside ink anyway via external functions, so ink ends up providing only prose flow.

options.name: Hybrid: TS engine + embedded ink fragments
options.summary: Custom TS engine owns graph, state, saves, and music; inkjs used purely as a text-realization library for prose-heavy scenes.
options.pros: Recovers ink's weave ergonomics exactly where the combinator DSL is weakest; keeps all state/saves/analysis benefits of the custom engine.
options.cons: Two toolchains, two authoring syntaxes, and a state-marshalling boundary to maintain; testing must cover the seam; premature complexity — only worth adopting later if prose volume proves the combinators painful.

options.name: Yarn Spinner (JS ports: yarn-bound / bondage.js)
options.summary: Node-based dialogue language with <<commands>> (natural music-cue fit) and declared-type variables; canonical implementation is C#/Unity, JS via community ports.
options.pros: Pleasant dialogue-first syntax; command syntax maps cleanly to music/stinger cues; variables have declared types.
options.cons: JS runtimes are second-tier community ports that lag the language spec — a foundation-level risk for a two-platform game; dialogue-first rather than stat/simulation-first; same external-state and analysis limitations as ink with a smaller ecosystem.

options.name: Twine / Twee (SugarCube, Harlowe)
options.summary: Passage-based hypertext format; story formats compile to self-contained HTML with DOM-bound runtimes.
options.pros: Huge authoring community; very fast to prototype pure hypertext; visual graph editing in the Twine app.
options.cons: Runtimes assume a DOM, so the terminal build requires writing a custom Twee interpreter — i.e., a custom engine with a worse, stringly, macro-based authoring format; poor programmatic authoring for an AI; weak state typing and no meaningful static analysis. Eliminated.

topPick: Custom TypeScript scene-graph DSL, wrapped in a pure deterministic engine (`advance(state, input) -> {state', output, events}`). Every hard requirement — typed stats, accumulated character memory, cross-run NG+ awareness, adaptive leitmotif music, save-schema versioning, and above all automated story testing — needs state and structure to be first-class *data*, which is exactly where ink, Twine, and Yarn are weakest. The runtime cost is small (hundreds of lines) and the payoff is unique to this option: compile-time checking of every scene/flag/stat/cue reference (the AI author's tightest feedback loop), exact graph analysis plus cheap 10k-run simulation because the engine is pure and headless, choiceLog-replay save migration that survives content patches, and music events emitted from the same state machine on web and terminal. Ink is the honorable runner-up on prose ergonomics alone — mitigate with a text-combinator layer, and keep the ink-fragments hybrid as a later escape valve rather than a starting point.
risks: 1) Terminal audio is the least-proven link: the mpv-IPC layered-stem approach needs an early spike (multi-process stem sync, mpv as an install prerequisite, Windows terminal behavior); commit to the graceful-degradation ladder (mpv layers → single-track cues → mute with on-screen '♪ now playing') before authoring the score. 2) Conditional reachability is only probabilistically verified — random/directed walkers can miss endings gated behind narrow stat corridors; decide the CI coverage bar and require a hand-authored golden path per ending as a hard gate. 3) Custom-DSL prose ergonomics: if the combinator layer proves too verbose for prose-dense scenes, adopt the ink-fragment hybrid deliberately rather than letting authors reach for `custom(fn)` closures, which silently erode static analysis. 4) choiceLog replay-migration can drift if any nondeterminism sneaks in (unseeded randomness, wall-clock, platform float differences) — enforce with the replay-equivalence property test from day one. 5) Web/CLI audio timing will never be bit-identical; define 'musically equivalent' acceptance criteria rather than chasing parity. 6) LLM character creation needs an offline/deterministic fallback as a launch requirement (terminal users, air-gapped play) and a decision on where API keys live for the web build (proxy vs. user-supplied). 7) Creator decisions needed: React vs. vanilla for web, whether saves are officially portable across platforms (recommended: yes), minimum terminal capabilities supported (color depth, Windows), and whether the music 'absence' motif idea is adopted since it couples score authoring to the twist."
